nodes.label: Hangul: Basic Vowels
nodes.description: Learn the foundational vertical and horizontal vowel strokes of the Korean alphabet.
nodes.cheatsheet: Korean is written in syllabic blocks. Master the basic vertical vowels: **ㅏ** (*a*), **ㅓ** (*eo*), **ㅣ** (*i*), and horizontal vowels: **ㅗ** (*o*), **ㅜ** (*u*), **ㅡ** (*eu*). Adding a short stroke creates a 'y' sound: **ㅑ** (*ya*), **ㅕ** (*yeo*), **ㅛ** (*yo*), **ㅠ** (*yu*). Vowels cannot stand alone; they use a silent circle filler **ㅇ** when starting a syllable (e.g., **아**).

nodes.label: Hangul: Basic Consonants
nodes.description: Master core consonant characters and learn how to pack them into syllable blocks.
nodes.cheatsheet: Learn basic shapes representing speech organs: **ㄱ** (*g/k*), **ㄴ** (*n*), **ㄷ** (*d/t*), **ㄹ** (*r/l*), **ㅁ** (*m*), **ㅂ** (*b/p*), **ㅅ** (*s*), **ㅇ** (*ng* when at bottom), **ㅈ** (*j*), **ㅎ** (*h*). Syllables pack consonants and vowels tightly into squares: Left-to-Right for vertical vowels (**가** / *ga*), Top-to-Bottom for horizontal vowels (**고** / *go*), and bottom placement for final consonants called Batchim (**간** / *gan*).

nodes.label: Greetings & Set Phrases
nodes.description: Master essential polite daily greetings, self-identifying phrases, and meeting transitions.
nodes.cheatsheet: Introduce your name: **이름이 무엇입니까?** (*What is your name?*) or politely **이름이 어떻게 되세요?**. Standard daily greeting: **안녕하세요** (*Hello / How are you?*). When leaving, if you are departing say **안녕히 계세요** (*Stay peacefully*). If you are staying and the other person is leaving, say **안녕히 가세요** (*Go pleasantly*). Meet someone for the first time: **처음 뵙겠습니다** (*Nice to meet you*).

nodes.label: Countries & Identity
nodes.description: Talk about nationalities, languages, and core family relationships.
nodes.cheatsheet: State nationality by adding **사람** (*person*) after a country name: **프랑스 사람** (*French person*), or use specific root words like **한국인** (*Korean*). Languages attach the suffix **-어** or **-국어** to the country: **한국어** (*Korean language*), **영어** (*English*). Basic family roots: **어머니** (*Mother*) and **아버지** (*Father*). Use **우리** (*our*) instead of 'my' for family: **우리 어머니** (*My/Our mother*).

nodes.label: Numbers, Hundreds & Thousands
nodes.description: Count higher figures into hundreds and thousands using Sino-Korean numbers, and learn basic fractions.
nodes.cheatsheet: Large figures use the Sino-Korean system. Hundreds are formed using **백** (100): 300 is **삼백**, 600 is **육백**. Thousands use **천** (1000): 3000 is **삼천**. Note that 10,000 shifts to a unique base unit **만** (*man*). Fractions place the denominator first, followed by the particle **분의** (*bun-ui*), then the numerator: 2/3 is written/spoken as **3분의 2** (*sam-bun-ui i*, literally 'of three parts, two').

nodes.label: Demonstratives (I-Geu-Jeo)
nodes.description: Identify objects using proximity-based pronouns and noun modifiers.
nodes.cheatsheet: Korean has a 3-tier proximity system: **이** (*this* - near me), **그** (*that* - near the listener / mentioned before), and **저** (*that over there* - far from both). To modify nouns directly, place them right before: **이 책** (*this book*). To use them as standalone pronouns, attach the word for thing (**것**): **이것** (*this thing*), **그것** (*that thing*), **저것** (*that thing over there*). Ask 'What is this?': **이것은 무엇입니까?**.

nodes.label: Colours & Descriptions
nodes.description: Use basic color vocabulary to modify and describe everyday objects.
nodes.cheatsheet: Primary color words change to adjective forms before nouns: **빨간** (*red*), **파란** (*blue*), **까만** (*black*), and **하얀** (*white*). For example: **빨간 사과** (*red apple*). Other common descriptive colors include **초록색** (*green*) and **노란** (*yellow*). When standing alone as nouns, they often append **-색** (*color*), such as **빨간색**.

nodes.label: General Counters & Quantity
nodes.description: Count items and quantities using Native Korean numbers and structural counting units.
nodes.cheatsheet: When counting objects, you *must* use Native Korean numbers (**하나, 둘, 셋...**) followed by a specific classifier noun. For general items/pieces, use **개** (*gae*): e.g., 'three books' is written as **책 세 권** (noun + number + classifier) or 'five apples' is **사과 다섯 개**. Ask 'How many?' using **몇 개?**.

nodes.label: Floors & Negative Numbers
nodes.description: Navigate building levels, floors, basements, and negative numbers using Sino-Korean digits.
nodes.cheatsheet: Count building levels using the Sino-Korean system paired with **층** (*cheung* / floor). First floor is **일층**, third floor is **삼층**. The basement levels use the prefix **지하** (*ji-ha*): B1 is **지하 일층**. For negative mathematical values or below-zero weather temperatures, utilize the prefix **마이너스** (*maineoseu*) or **영하** (*yeong-ha* / sub zero), such as **영하 오 도** (*minus 5 degrees*).

nodes.label: Buildings & Spatial Relations
nodes.description: Identify common spatial structures and describe basic physical layouts with location particles.
nodes.cheatsheet: Common locations: **건물** (*building*) and **역** (*station*). Position objects using spatial nouns followed by the location particle **-에** (*at/in*): **위** (*on/above*), **아래 / 밑** (*under*), **앞** (*in front of*), and **뒤** (*behind*). Example: **책상 위에** (*on top of the desk*), **건물 앞에** (*in front of the building*).

nodes.label: Existence & Amount of People
nodes.description: Express presence, absence, and existence of objects or groups of people.
nodes.cheatsheet: Korean uses **있다** (*itda*) for 'there is/are / to have' and **없다** (*eoptda*) for 'there is not/are not / to not have'. Example: **방에 두 명이 있습니다** (*There are two people in the room*). To count people, use Native Korean numbers followed by the specialized person counter **명** (*myeong*) or polite **분** (*bun*): **한 명** (*one person*), **두 명** (*two people*). Ask 'How many people?': **몇 명?**.

nodes.label: Relative Time: Day, Week, Month, Year
nodes.description: Anchor timelines cleanly across relative days, weeks, months, and years.
nodes.cheatsheet:
Timeline grid:
Days: **그저께** (*day before yesterday*) → **어제** (*yesterday*) → **오늘** (*today*) → **내일** (*tomorrow*) → **모레** (*day after tomorrow*).
Weeks: **지난주** (*last week*) → **이번 주** (*this week*) → **다음 주** (*next week*).
Months: **지난달** (*last month*) / **이번 달** (*this month*) / **다음 달** (*next month*).
Years: **작년** (*last year*) / **올해** (*this year*) / **내년** (*next year*).

nodes.label: Specific Time: This Morning & Next Week
nodes.description: Isolate immediate parts of a day or adjacent calendar blocks.
nodes.cheatsheet: Isolate specific windows of time like **오늘 아침** (*this morning*) or focus forward to **다음 주** (*next week*). Relative standalone temporal adverbs answering 'when' (like **오늘 아침**, **내일**, **어제**) do not require the time particle **-에**, whereas specific clock times or days of the week do.

nodes.label: Months & Days of the Month
nodes.description: Identify calendar months and navigate numeric dates of the month using Sino-Korean structures.
nodes.cheatsheet: Months are simple: attach **월** (*wol*) to Sino-Korean numbers: January is **일월**, February is **이월**, June is **유월** (note exception), October is **시월** (note exception). Days of the month attach **일** (*il*) to Sino-Korean numbers: e.g., the 4th is **사일**. 'May 4th' is written/spoken as **5월 4일** (*o-wol sa-il*). The first day can also be referred to as **초하루** or simply **일일**.

nodes.label: Year & Month Durations
nodes.description: Calculate spans and durations of time using years and month blocks.
nodes.cheatsheet: To express spans of time, use Sino-Korean numbers with duration units: **년** (*nyeon* / year) or **개월** (*gae-wol* / month duration). Alternatively, Native Korean numbers can pair with **달** (*dal* / month duration): e.g., **삼 개월** or **세 달** both mean 'for three months'. You can add **동안** (*dongan* / during/for) for explicit emphasis: **세 달 동안** (*for a duration of three months*). Ask 'How many months?': **몇 개월?** or **몇 달?**.

nodes.label: Asking 'When' (Onje)
nodes.description: Formulate open-ended temporal questions about future, present, or past events.
nodes.cheatsheet: Use the absolute question word **언제** (*When?*) to query schedules. Like relative time words, it stands cleanly in the sentence without needing destination particles. Example: **언제 한국에 갑니까?** (*When are you going to Korea?*).

nodes.label: Meals & Food Types
nodes.description: Categorize daily meals and basic dish selections.
nodes.cheatsheet: Categorize daylight food frames using words that double as both the mealtime and the food itself: **아침** or **아침 식사** (*breakfast*), **점심** (*lunch*), and **저녁** (*dinner*). Identify core cultural staples like **밥** (*rice/meal*), **김치** (*kimchi*), and **불고기** (*bulgogi*).

nodes.label: Consumptive Actions (Eat & Drink)
nodes.description: Utilize active consumption roots for eating and drinking with object markers.
nodes.cheatsheet: Core verb dictionary roots: **먹다** (*to eat*) and **마시다** (*to drink*). In the standard polite present tense, they become **먹어요** (*I eat / am eating*) and **마셔요** (*I drink / am drinking*). Mark your definite or indefinite objects clearly with the object particles **-을 / -를**: **차를 마셔요** (*I am drinking tea*), **밥을 먹어요** (*I am eating rice*).

nodes.label: Verb Conjugation & Tenses
nodes.description: Conjugate actions into polite affirmative, negative, past, and exploratory desire states.
nodes.cheatsheet:
Polite informal quadrant using **먹다** (to eat) for 'I':
Affirmative: **먹어요** (*I eat / am eating*)
Negative: Place **안** before the verb: **안 먹어요** (*I don't eat*)
Past: **먹었어요** (*I ate*)
Desire: Attach the construction **-고 싶다** to the verb stem: **먹고 싶어요** (*I want to eat*). Twist to a question: **먹고 싶어요?** (*Do you want to eat?*).

nodes.label: Conjunctions (And & Then)
nodes.description: Link phrases together or order successive events smoothly.
nodes.cheatsheet: Use conversational connectors: **그리고** (*and*) to link separate sentences, or connect nouns in a list using **하고** (*and*). To chain distinct actions sequentially over time ('and then / after that'), use the sentence starter **그러고 나서** or append the clitic **-고** directly to the first verb stem (e.g., **먹고 마셔요** / *I eat and then drink*).

nodes.label: Frequency of Verbs
nodes.description: Quantify how often routines occur using positive and absolute negative adverbs.
nodes.cheatsheet: Scale your routines: **자주** (*often*), **가끔** (*sometimes*), **별로** (*not very often*), and **전혀** (*never*). Note: Both **별로** and **전혀** absolute-trigger a negative verb form following them. Example: **불고기를 전혀 안 먹어요** (*I never eat bulgogi*).

nodes.label: Transportation & Commuting
nodes.description: Describe modes of travel and ask others how they commute to work or school.
nodes.cheatsheet: Ask 'How do you go?' using **어떻게 가요?**. Mark vehicles with the instrumental direction particle **-으로 / -로**: **비행기로** (*by plane*), **차로** (*by car*), **지하철로** (*by subway*). For walking, use the active adverbial gerund phrase **걸어서** (*by walking/on foot*).

nodes.label: Sports & Complex Preferences
nodes.description: Express likes and dislikes regarding sports, distinguishing between 'what' and 'what kind'.
nodes.cheatsheet: To ask 'What sports do you like?', use the modifier **무슨** (*what*): **무슨 운동을 좋아해요?**. To ask 'What kind of sports do you like?', use **어떤 운동을 좋아해요?** (*What kind of / which type of sports...*). Target your preferences using the verb **좋아해요** (*I like*) or **싫어해요** (*I dislike*), marking the sport with the object particle **-을 / -를**.
